talk_title: LINE1 ORF1p as a pan‑cancer biomarker and ultra‑sensitive single‑molecule detection assay
tumor_types: head and neck squamous cell carcinoma

biomarkers.name: LINE1 ORF1p plasma level
biomarkers.assay: ultrasensitive single‑molecule ELISA (microwell) / Mosaic flow cytometry assay
biomarkers.finding: Detected in ~50% of cancer patients (n≈600) and 0/406 healthy controls; early‑stage ovarian cancer sensitivity increased to 83% with second‑gen assay

models.name: healthy plasma cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: n=406 healthy individuals, age up to 95

models.name: cancer plasma cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: ≈600 cancer patients across multiple tumor types

models.name: ovarian cancer pilot
models.genotype_or_key_features: 50 ovarian cancer patients (including 6 early stage) vs 50 healthy

models.name: gastroesophageal monitoring cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: 19 gastroesophageal cancer patients with serial samples

MOA_summary: LINE1 ORF1p is de‑repressed in cancer and high‑risk precursors, producing a tumor‑specific protein detectable in plasma via ultra‑sensitive single‑molecule sandwich assays; assay sensitivity improves with custom nanobody pairs and bar‑coded Mosaic detection, enabling early‑stage cancer detection and real‑time monitoring of treatment response.
key_findings: LINE1 ORF1p is expressed in >99% of examined carcinomas and high‑risk precursors across organ systems.
First‑generation single‑molecule assay detected ORF1p in 50% of cancer plasma samples while remaining undetectable in 402/406 healthy controls.
Second‑generation nanobody‑based assays increased early‑stage ovarian cancer sensitivity to 83% without loss of specificity.
Adding ORF1p to a CA125+HE4 panel raised AUC from 0.94 to 0.98 in a pilot ovarian cancer cohort.
Serial ORF1p levels perfectly segregated responders from non‑responders in a gastroesophageal cancer monitoring cohort (p<0.001).
Mosaic assay achieved detection in 100% of gastroesophageal trial samples, outperforming Simoa.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Assay specificity is high because ORF1p is absent in normal adult tissues and not induced by aging, chronic liver disease, autoimmune conditions, or COVID‑19.
Limited tissue‑of‑origin information; positive signal indicates presence of LINE1‑expressing tumor but not its anatomical source.

combination_strategies.rationale: Orthogonal protein biomarker for ovarian cancer
combination_strategies.phenotype: AUC increased to 0.98 when combined with ORF1p

combination_strategies.rationale: Standard ovarian cancer marker; improves detection when added to ORF1p

open_questions: What is the limit of detection for early‑stage, low‑tumor‑burden cancers across different tumor types?
Can ORF1p levels be quantitatively linked to tumor burden and used for longitudinal monitoring?
What biofluids (stool, Pap smear, CSF) can reliably preserve ORF1p for detection?
How does ORF1p detection compare and combine with ctDNA or methylation‑based MCED assays?

talk_title: Naturally occurring antibodies as biomarkers for early cancer detection and outcome prediction

speaker.name: Qi Dang
speaker.affiliation: University of Texas MD Anderson Cancer Center

biomarkers.name: autoantibodies to NY-ESO-1
biomarkers.finding: higher prevalence in cancer patients vs controls; AUC modest but improves when combined with other markers

biomarkers.name: autoantibodies to p53
biomarkers.finding: outperforms CA125 alone for ovarian cancer detection; combination improves AUC

biomarkers.name: anti‑interferon antibodies
biomarkers.finding: associated with better response to checkpoint inhibitor therapy across melanoma, NSCLC, RCC

biomarkers.name: viral exposure antibody signature
biomarkers.finding: high viral antibody score predicts poorer survival in HCC patients receiving immunotherapy

models.name: lung cancer antibody study
models.genotype_or_key_features: cases, benign lung disease, healthy controls

models.name: pancreatic cancer autoantibody panel
models.genotype_or_key_features: PDAC patients vs controls; pooled serum arrays

models.name: HCC viral exposure signature cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: at‑risk individuals, HCC patients, longitudinal samples

models.name: checkpoint inhibitor response autoantibody cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: melanoma, NSCLC, RCC patients treated with ICI

clinical_data.metric: AUC (p53 autoantibody + CA125 vs CA125 alone)

clinical_data.value: higher than AFP (exact value not specified)

MOA_summary: Naturally occurring autoantibodies arise against tumor‑associated antigens (e.g., NY‑ESO‑1, p53, MMP14, LINE1 ORF1p) and viral antigens; high‑throughput protein arrays and PhIP‑seq identify antibody signatures that can differentiate cancer patients from controls, predict early disease, and correlate with immunotherapy outcomes.
key_findings: Single autoantibody markers show modest AUCs; panels of multiple antibodies substantially improve diagnostic performance.
p53 autoantibodies combined with CA125 outperform CA125 alone for ovarian cancer detection.
Anti‑interferon antibodies are enriched in responders to checkpoint inhibition across several tumor types, suggesting a predictive biomarker.
A viral exposure antibody signature predicts poorer survival in HCC patients receiving immunotherapy and rises longitudinally before HCC diagnosis.
IgG deposition is abundant on tumor cells in tissue sections, and cloned autoantibodies (e.g., against MMP14) can enhance phagocytosis and NK‑cell ADCC.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Autoantibody panels may be limited by tumor heterogeneity and lack of tissue‑of‑origin specificity.
High viral antibody burden correlates with worse immunotherapy outcomes, indicating a potential resistance-associated immune profile.

combination_strategies.rationale: Orthogonal protein biomarker for ovarian cancer

combination_strategies.phenotype: AUC increase when combined with p53 autoantibody

open_questions: What is the optimal number and selection of autoantibodies to achieve high sensitivity and specificity across cancer types?
Can autoantibody signatures be integrated with other MCED modalities (e.g., ctDNA, methylation) for a unified early‑detection test?
What is the mechanistic basis for anti‑interferon antibodies predicting better ICI response?

talk_title: Unbiased autoantibody atlas for early cancer detection

biomarkers.name: autoantibodies against PD-L1 chaperone protein
biomarkers.finding: associated with improved overall survival in HCC patients (higher levels in responders vs non‑responders)

MOA_summary: The study builds a large-scale atlas of autoantibodies by profiling serum from liver disease cohorts using human protein arrays and FIP‑seq, revealing distinct autoantibody signatures that separate HCC from cirrhosis/hepatitis and identifying antibodies that correlate with clinical outcomes.
key_findings: Principal component analysis of autoantibody profiles segregates HCC patients from cirrhosis and hepatitis cohorts.
Heat‑map clustering shows a distinct blue cluster of HCC samples versus red cluster of non‑cancer liver disease.
Autoantibodies against a PD‑L1 chaperone protein are enriched in HCC patients with longer overall survival.
The planned CRUK Grand Challenge atlas will expand to >16,000 individuals across high‑risk groups (genetic predisposition, heavy smokers, obese, centenarians, twins).
Baseline autoantibody patterns are being evaluated as predictors of response to checkpoint inhibitor neoadjuvant therapy.
open_questions: Do microbial‑derived autoantibodies contribute causally to HCC development or are they bystanders?
Will pre‑existing anti‑tumor autoantibodies affect the biodistribution or efficacy of therapeutic antibodies?
